text: The surgeon encounters a challenge during dissection as the cord lipoma is connected to retroperitoneal lymph nodes, affecting the direct and indirect spaces.
The surgeon creates a wide peritoneal flap, beginning with medial dissection, to expose the hernia site.
The surgeon navigates pre-transverse salus, attempting to access the pre-peritoneal space laterally.
The surgical field shows minimal presence of an indirect hernia sac.
The surgeon identifies the pubic bone, which is visible within the surgical field during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon works on parietalizing the cord structures, carefully separating them from surrounding tissues in the absence of a hernia sac.
The surgeon sweeps the cord structures laterally, creating space in the surgical field. The absence of a hernia sac is noted.
The surgeon continues to work in the exposed area, but the surgical field begins to visibly collapse, limiting the working space.
The surgeon observes the vas deferens in the surgical field but struggles to maintain exposure due to collapsing tissue or workspace limitations.
The surgeon uses a sponge to assist with retraction, preventing tearing of the surrounding fat tissue with the left hand.
The surgeon observes a large ball of fat located to the right, which is likely associated with the patient's symptomatic hernia.
The surgeon begins to reduce the hernia, manipulating the tissue to reposition it within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon attempts to visualize the chord structures but struggles to maintain a clear view due to collapsing tissue around the surgical area.
The surgeon observes the abdomen re-insufflate, indicating the restoration of proper gas pressure within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon parietalizes the cord structures, identifying the vas deferens in an area without a hernia sac.
The surgeon locates the spermatic cord within the surgical field.
The surgeon begins to reduce a large lipoma, which is visibly prominent in the surgical field.
The surgeon encounters lymph node tissue during the reduction, which poses a challenge as it obstructs the placement of the mesh.
The surgeon attempts to resect as much of the lipoma as possible to prevent any tissue from herniating behind the mesh in the inguinal canal.
The surgeon ensures that no obstructive tissue prevents the mesh from properly seating against the right myopectineal orifice (MPO).
The surgeon continues to mobilize a large lipoma from the indirect space, carefully evaluating its extent as it is gradually freed from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon isolates and removes the remaining portion of the lipoma, which is visibly extending and connected to the lateral aspect of the spermatic cord.
The surgeon carefully protects the cord structures while observing additional tissue extending upwards, indicating further dissection may be required.
The surgeon amputates the remaining portion of the lipoma at the specified level, ensuring complete removal from the surgical field.
The surgeon observes the remaining retroperitoneal nodes, considering their potential impact on the placement of the mesh within the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the reduction of the hernia, opting to leave the tissue intact while finishing the parietalization and establishing the right medial preperitoneal overlay (MPO).
The surgeon reveals a large pocket within the surgical field and uses a 16x12 ProGrip mesh to address the hernia.
The surgeon sutures the flap closed, completing the procedure.
